description: A natural body orbiting a planet is a 'lunei'. In ordinary speech near Earth, the bare word refers to Earth's moon. Moonlight is reflected, and the apparent phases follow the changing geometry among moon, planet, and sun.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip at the alveolar ridge for lateral 'l' and release along its sides into high-back rounded 'u'. Set the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n' and carry nasal voice into stressed middle-height front 'e'. Preserve the final hiatus as the tongue rises into high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The shared 'lu.n' opening makes the relationship with 'lunoa' (month) audible. The two words then take different vowels and endings, keeping the lunar association memorable without turning the shared sound into an affix.
usage_notes: Named moons retain their source names when identity matters. 'lunei' supplies the ordinary class, while 'lunisa' (tide) names the recurring water movement to which the moon contributes.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Observed lunar phases have helped organise calendars, night travel, fishing, and ceremonies in many distinct traditions. The particular reckoning and its meaning belong to the people who maintain it.

semantic_domains.nature: a natural body orbiting a planet, especially Earth's moon in local speech
semantic_domains.temporal: a visible cycle used in lunar months and other local reckonings
